figures.gloss: The Wise Lord, uncreated and supreme — the one luminous ordering god from whom Truth proceeds and who fathers the Bounteous Immortals as facets of his own mind.
figures.facet: The shard where the sky-father becomes an ethical absolute: not the strongest god but the wholly GOOD one, who reigns by Truth rather than by storm or sky alone.
figures.provenance: Gathas of Zarathushtra; later Avesta. 'Ahura' is the cognate of Vedic 'asura'.

figures.gloss: Truth and right Order — the cosmic righteousness the world is built upon and to which every soul and even the gods are bound; the opposite of Druj, the Lie.
figures.facet: Where impersonal cosmic law becomes a moral demand: Asa is not merely how the cosmos turns but the ethical Truth a human being must actively choose and uphold.
figures.provenance: Avestan asa, Old Persian arta; from PIE *h2r-to, the same root as Vedic rta.

figures.gloss: The Destroying Spirit, the hostile mind who chose the Lie at the beginning — the principled author of evil, decay and death, locked in cosmic combat with the good creation.
figures.facet: The world's first fully ethical Devil: evil here is not chaos or shadow but a free moral CHOICE, an adversary co-primordial in will though doomed to lose in time.
figures.provenance: Avesta; elaborated in the Pahlavi Bundahishn. The classic source of principled cosmic dualism.

figures.gloss: The Bounteous Spirit, the creative and holy mind of Ahura Mazda, the twin of Angra Mainyu — the good half of the primal pair of choosing wills.
figures.facet: The twin that refuses to be a balanced complement: paired with its opposite like Yin and Yang, yet the pairing is WAR, not harmony — the moment complementarity fractures into dualism.
figures.provenance: Gathas, Yasna 30: the two primal Spirits who chose.

figures.gloss: Lord of the covenant and the wide pastures, all-seeing guardian of oaths who rides before the sun — the divine enforcer of the bond between persons and between worlds.
figures.facet: The god of the SWORN WORD: where the solar eye is moralised into the unsleeping witness of every contract, the one a liar cannot escape.
figures.provenance: Mihr Yasht. Avestan Mithra is the tier-1 cognate of Vedic Mitra; later the namesake of Roman Mithras.

figures.gloss: The first king and shepherd of mortals, who built the vara to shelter the seeds of all living things against the killing winter — a Noah before the flood, a Golden-Age sovereign undone by pride.
figures.facet: The first-ancestor as ARK-BUILDER: not the man humanity descends from by death, but the king who preserves life through catastrophe, then forfeits his glory by lying.
figures.provenance: Vendidad fargard 2; Shahnameh. Avestan Yima is the tier-1 cognate of Vedic Yama.

figures.gloss: The Making Wonderful — the final renovation when evil is purged, the dead are raised, a river of molten metal refines all souls, and the world is restored to deathless perfection.
figures.facet: Where cyclic time bends into an ARROW: not eternal return but a single decisive end, the first true eschatology — history goes somewhere and finishes good.
figures.provenance: Avesta; Bundahishn and Zand-i Wahman Yasn.

date.note: The final renovation of the world — eschatological future.

figures.gloss: The world-saviour to come, born of Zarathushtra's preserved seed and a virgin, who will lead the final battle, raise the dead and usher in the renovation.
figures.facet: The redeemer as FUTURE certainty: a saviour not yet born but guaranteed by the structure of time itself — the template behind the awaited messiah.
figures.provenance: Farvardin Yasht; Pahlavi sources. Argued to influence later messianic expectation.

date.note: The Zoroastrian end-time savior — a future, not a past; lives to the RIGHT of now.

figures.gloss: The Bounteous Immortals — Good Mind, Truth, Dominion, Devotion, Wholeness and Immortality — six radiant aspects of Ahura Mazda's nature, each guarding one realm of creation.
figures.facet: Monotheism that still breathes as a pantheon: the One refracted into a council of virtues, divine persons who are also abstractions you are meant to BECOME.
figures.provenance: Gathas and Younger Avesta.

figures.gloss: The sacred Fire, son of Ahura Mazda — not worshipped as an idol but tended as the visible presence of Truth and the purifying instrument of the divine.
figures.facet: Where fire is neither devoured offering nor stolen gift but a kept WITNESS: the undying flame as the focal point of right worship, purity made visible.
figures.provenance: Avesta; Atash Niyayesh. Cognate root with Vedic and Latin fire-words is debated, not secure.

figures.gloss: The priest-poet (zaotar) of the Gathas, seventeen hymns in an archaic Avestan meter, who broke with the daeva-cult of the old Iranian pantheon to preach Ahura Mazda as sole uncreated Wise Lord and set the moral cosmos on the single choice between Asa (Truth) and Druj (the Lie).
figures.facet: The tradition's own hinge point: Ahura Mazda's ethical absolutism, the individual judgment at the Chinvat Bridge, and the promised Saoshyant born of his line all proceed from one reforming voice whose dates the tradition itself cannot fix within eight centuries.
figures.provenance: The Gathas (Yasna 28-34, 43-51, 53), attributed to Zarathushtra; his historicity and dates (linguistic evidence points to c. 1200-1000 BCE, later Zoroastrian tradition to the 6th century BCE) remain debated.

figures.gloss: Aredvi Sura Anahita, 'the damp, strong, undefiled' — the great yazata of the cosmic river flowing from the world-mountain to the sea, purifier of the seed of men and the womb and milk of women, invoked by kings for victory and by the whole land for its waters.
figures.facet: Where the tradition's ethics run on abstraction, Anahita is the shard of concrete cult: statue-images, temple fires, and Achaemenid and Sasanian royal investiture rites are attested for her as for no other Zoroastrian power — divinity you could see poured in silver at Ecbatana.
figures.provenance: Aban Yasht (Yasht 5); Achaemenid inscriptions of Artaxerxes II, who reintroduced her cult statues; Berossus reports the cult program that also linked her image to Ishtar's.

figures.gloss: The golden plant pressed and prepared at the center of the Yasna liturgy, and the divinity of that plant at once — its juice, drunk by priest and worshipper, confers strength, healing, fertility and inspired insight; mythically the first to press Haoma was Vivahvant, Yima's father.
figures.facet: A rare licit ecstasy inside a famously sober religion: where the Gathas warn against the old intoxicant cults, Haoma survives and is sanctified precisely by being brought inside the liturgy and given a will of its own to consent to the pressing.
figures.provenance: Yasna 9-11 (the Hom Yasht); central to Zoroastrian ritual practice since the Achaemenid era, still performed by Parsi and Irani priests.

figures.gloss: The Bridge of the Separator (Cinvato Peretu), which every soul must cross on the fourth dawn after death: broad and easy for the just, narrow as a razor's edge for the wicked, who fall from it into the House of Lies.
figures.facet: The individual judgment made structural: unlike the collective Frashokereti at history's end, the Bridge judges one soul at a time, met there by its own daena — its conscience made visible as a radiant maiden or a hag shaped by its own deeds.
figures.provenance: Yasna 46.10-11; Vendidad 19; the Hadokht Nask's fuller narrative of the soul's three nights after death.

figures.gloss: Azi Dahaka, the three-headed, three-mouthed serpent-tyrant — mightiest of the creatures of the Lie, a usurping king with snakes growing from his shoulders in the Shahnameh's telling (as Zahhak), fed daily on human brains until overthrown.
figures.facet: The one monster the Zoroastrian imagination allows itself: elsewhere evil is abstract will, but Azi Dahaka gives the Lie a body — and even that body cannot simply be killed, only bound, since final defeat is reserved for the end of time.
figures.provenance: Zamyad Yasht (Yasht 19); Vendidad 1; Ferdowsi's Shahnameh (as Zahhak/Zahhak-Dahag), bound by Fereydun beneath Mount Damavand.

figures.gloss: Divine Hearkening-Obedience: the yazata who first sacrificed to Ahura Mazda with the sacred words, who patrols by night against the demons club in hand, and who meets the soul at the Chinvat Bridge on the third night after death to guide it across.
figures.facet: Obedience elevated to its own godhood: where other traditions make the underworld guide a trickster or a stranger, Sraosha is simply the correct RESPONSE to divine order, personified — the ritual attitude itself walking the soul home.
figures.provenance: Yasna 57 (Srosh Yasht); invoked in the daily liturgy more than almost any other yazata; his epithet 'tanu-manthra', 'whose body is the sacred formula.'

figures.gloss: Smiting Victory: the yazata invoked against every obstruction of the Lie, who descends upon his worshipper in ten successive forms — wind, bull, horse, camel, boar, youth, raven, ram, wild goat, and armed warrior — the last binding on the whole apparatus of war.
figures.facet: Victory itself made polymorphous: rather than one warrior-god, Verethragna is a sequence of embodied advantages, so triumph in the tradition is less a single champion's deed than a cascade of forms borrowed as needed.
figures.provenance: Bahram Yasht (Yasht 14); namesake of the Bahram fire, highest grade of Zoroastrian temple fire; Avestan Verethragna and Vedic Vrtrahan (an epithet of Indra) are the tier-1 Indo-Iranian cognate, 'smiter of resistance.'

figures.gloss: The uncreated, pre-existent guardian spirits of all beings — human, animal, even the yazatas themselves — who at creation chose to descend into material bodies and fight the Lie rather than remain safe and unincarnate; the dead rejoin them and are honored collectively at Farvardigan.
figures.facet: Ancestor-cult folded into cosmology: the Fravashis are not merely the surviving dead but a metaphysical category prior to birth, so every act of ancestral reverence in Zoroastrianism is also an act of honoring a choice made before the world existed.
figures.provenance: Farvardin Yasht (Yasht 13); the Fravardigan/Muktad observance for the dead in the last ten days of the Zoroastrian year.

figures.gloss: The Lie: cosmic falsehood, disorder and death, the principle every demon (daeva) and every sin instantiates; a person is drugvant, 'possessed of the Lie,' or ashavan, 'possessed of Truth' — the tradition's foundational moral sorting of all beings.
figures.facet: Where many traditions treat falsehood as an absence — ignorance, shadow, the mere lack of truth — Druj is a positive, active force with its own partisans and its own army; the Lie does not merely fail to be true, it fights.
figures.provenance: Passim in the Gathas and Younger Avesta, always paired against Asa; the drugvant/ashavan distinction structures Zoroastrian ethical and funerary practice alike.

figures.gloss: The Zoroastrian sacred corpus, composed in the otherwise-unattested Avestan language across many centuries: the Gathas at its ancient core (Zarathushtra's own hymns), the Yasna liturgy that frames them, Yashts of praise to individual yazatas, and the Vendidad's purity law.
figures.facet: A scripture assembled backward from ruin: only a fraction of the Sasanian-era Avesta — itself already a rescue-compilation of older oral material — survived the Arab conquest, so the roster's own provenance lines all point back to a text smaller than the religion it anchors.
figures.provenance: Surviving manuscripts date from the 13th century CE; the Sasanian 21-nask Avesta it descends from is described (and its loss lamented) in Zoroastrian tradition itself, e.g. the Denkard.

figures.gloss: The 'tower of silence': a raised, roofless stone enclosure where the dead are exposed to sun, weather and carrion birds rather than buried, burned or given to water — because a corpse, seized by the corpse-demon (Nasu), would otherwise defile earth, fire or water, the good creations Zoroastrians are bound to protect.
figures.facet: Funerary architecture built entirely from purity logic rather than sentiment: the practice is not about honoring the dead but about quarantining death itself from contaminating a world whose elements are held to be intrinsically, physically sacred.
figures.provenance: Vendidad, especially fargard 6 and 8, on corpse-impurity law; dakhmas historically used by Parsis (notably Mumbai's Towers of Silence) and Iranian Zoroastrians, now declining with urbanization and vulture collapse.

figures.gloss: The star-yazata of the rain, identified with Sirius/Tir, who each year takes the form of a white horse and battles the drought-demon Apaosha in his black-horse form at the cosmic sea Vourukasha; on the third attempt, aided by the sacrifices of the faithful, Tishtrya wins and the rains fall.
figures.facet: Chaoskampf turned into an annual utility bill: the fight is not a single founding act but a recurring contest whose outcome depends partly on whether humans have offered enough worship — cosmology that makes ritual an actual condition of rainfall.
figures.provenance: Tishtar Yasht (Yasht 8); the Bundahishn's cosmological elaboration of the Tishtrya-Apaosha combat.

figures.gloss: Yazata of Justice, who holds the golden scale at the Chinvat Bridge and weighs a soul's good thoughts, words and deeds against its evil ones with perfect, unbribable accuracy — standing alongside Mithra and Sraosha as the third judge of the dead.
figures.facet: Justice stripped of mercy and of malice alike: Rashnu neither pleads for the soul nor accuses it, but simply renders the arithmetic of a life exact — judgment as pure measurement rather than verdict.
figures.provenance: Rashn Yasht (Yasht 12); named with Mithra and Sraosha as the triad presiding at the Chinvat crossing in the Hadokht Nask.

figures.gloss: Aeshma, 'Wrath' or 'Fury' — the daeva of violent rage and instability, chief among the forces of the Lie, invoked against Sraosha (Obedience) as his direct opponent in the Gathic material.
figures.facet: Evil localized as a mood: where Angra Mainyu is cosmic and structural, Aeshma is the Lie experienced from the inside — the specific passion of blind fury that makes a person, even briefly, drugvant.
figures.provenance: Gathas (Yasna 29, 32, 48) name Aeshma among the earliest-attested daevas; his name passes directly into Second Temple Jewish demonology as Ashmedai/Asmodeus (Book of Tobit), a documented linguistic and conceptual borrowing.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the active, structurally necessary principle of darkness and opposition that eternally battles the light in the material realm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Supreme deities of pure light whose original perfect creations are fractured by an ontological conflict with darkness.
GOLD SEAM: The eschatological culmination where matter is fundamentally transmuted, burned clean of all corruption, and rendered permanently immortal.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The prophesied savior figure who will emerge at the end of the current age to execute a total world-reversal.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The eschatological concept of a broken world being perfectly restored and cleansed of its corrupted history.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Steiner explicitly borrowed the Zoroastrian adversary of light, repurposing him as the spirit of earthly mechanization and materialism.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A purifying, non-destructive spiritual fire conceived as a literal substance that transmutes cosmic decay into divine order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An emanationist council of divine attributes or 'Rays', acting collectively as the supreme executive branch of God.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are prophesied eschatological figures arriving at the darkest age to completely dismantle evil and renovate the earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Primordial antagonists whose fragmentation or emanations infect the material world with structural deceit and suffering across successive epochs.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A permanent, physical renovation of the earthly realm that completely breaks the cycle of decay, instating eternal equality and justice.
GOLD SEAM: Bahá'u'lláh is explicitly identified as the fulfillment of the Zoroastrian prophecy of the Saoshyant who brings the final renovation of the world.
GOLD SEAM: The realization of the Most Great Peace is the social and spiritual equivalent of the Zoroastrian 'making wonderful' of the world—the final victory of light.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A radical purification and rebooting of the world, though Nyepi performs this in an endless cycle rather than as a singular eschatological event.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are eschatological saviors who arrive specifically to violently purge the world of falsehood and restore a righteous empire for the chosen.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Edom functions as the systemic, geopolitical embodiment of deceit and evil, mirroring the Zoroastrian cosmic architect of chaos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a profound, physical renovation of the earth where the wicked are finally subjugated and the righteous inherit a tangible, purified world.

Speculative: both are deathless-first-king myths whose loss of an original immortal condition founds mortality — Yima's fall from grace and the cut mu-cord as the end of corpse-less kingship. A loose thematic parallel across the plateau and Iran; no contact claimed, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An uncreated truth and cosmic order that stands as the ultimate referent for righteous action and knowledge.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A systemic force of decay and falsehood that historically corrupted the pure original state of humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Vedic Yajna fire and the Avestan Yasna (Atar) fire share a direct, proven Indo-Iranian origin as the purifying ritual center.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are prophesied saviors destined to intervene in the suffering world to lead humanity toward final restoration, echoing the Maitreya figure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an active, holy mentality or spiritual force that continuously guides the practitioner toward cosmic order and the absolute good.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A documented historical and linguistic syncretism where the Iranian supreme deity was adapted into the Mongol-Buryat sky pantheon via Sogdian trade networks.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A final, cosmic era of renovation designed not to destroy the world, but to restore and unite a fractured humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are eschatological saviors expected to return at the end of time to banish oppressors and restore a golden age.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancient figures associated with a primordial golden age who retreat, promising their utopian realm will eventually encompass the earth again.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are primordial, ancient kings associated with a lost golden age who established metaphysical paths to immortality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The definitive, final transformation of the cosmos from an era of conflict and suffering into a perfected epoch of harmony.
GOLD SEAM: A martial figure wielding divine weapons who is less a god of war than a guardian of oaths, truth, and contracts.
GOLD SEAM: The domestic fire revered as a pure, observing presence that acts as a messenger between the human household and supreme celestial justice.
GOLD SEAM: The cosmic adversary who seeks to ruin the supreme deity's good creation through deception, though Christian theology denies him co-eternal status.
GOLD SEAM: The eschatological vision of a final bodily resurrection and a remade, purified world entered Second Temple Judaism and early Christianity through Persian Zoroastrian contact.
GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme, uncreated luminous emanations that structure the divine will and oversee the cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are a specialized, immortal hierarchy of beneficent entities who assist the Supreme Being in maintaining cosmic order and guiding humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Both are solar deities whose all-seeing light makes them the ultimate guarantors of covenants and oaths in greater Iran.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A dualistic dark twin who introduces disease and hostile animals into a pristine creation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The prophesied world savior who arrives to permanently end the cosmic cycle of decay and renovate the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The active, structural principle of cosmic truth and righteousness that inherently opposes degeneration and falsehood.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A high-stakes, apocalyptic renovation of the universe that destroys accumulated cosmic corruption and permanently institutes a perfected order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The great founding patriarch who leads humanity's ancestors to their destined home before the closing of the primordial era.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fully crystallized being or force that possesses true will and immortal presence, but applies it purely in opposition to cosmic harmony and objective conscience.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The final world-savior who leads humanity into the ultimate renovation of the earth and the resurrection of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An apocalyptic, physical renovation of the universe that permanently purges evil and restores a perfect, primeval state.
Both are the principled source of a flawed world, but Ahriman is a free moral evil opposing a good creation, while Yaldabaoth is IGNORANCE wearing the mask of God — malice versus blindness.

Mani consciously drew on Zoroastrian dualism; both pit two primal principles against each other — but Iran's good Spirit defends a good creation, while Mani condemns matter itself as Darkness. Attested borrowing, radicalised.

Mani named Zoroaster among his forerunners; the Light-Darkness war is built on the Iranian template of co-primordial good and evil — historical descent of doctrine, then pushed past its source into anti-cosmic dualism.
Mani placed himself in the prophetic succession that includes Zoroaster, whose tradition awaits the Saoshyant — the Seal of the Prophets claiming and completing the Iranian revelation he gathered in.

Light hidden and tended within the world is shared, but Iran's Atar is the kept outward flame of Truth while the gnostic spark is the SECRET inner light asleep in matter — public witness versus buried captive.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent profound adversarial forces; Asmoday stems specifically from the Zoroastrian Aeshma Daeva, a lieutenant of Angra Mainyu.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Legendary kings who ruled over demons and humanity alike during a golden age before falling from grace.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are absolute cosmic adversaries who actively attempt to plunge the created world into darkness by attacking the light.
GOLD SEAM: The elevation of physical and spiritual purity—mediated by natural elements like water or fire—as the absolute highest cosmic law.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are a sacred septet of divine emanations governing aspects of creation on behalf of a supreme Lord.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a fundamental, underlying cosmic matrix of cause, effect, and truthful action that even deities are subject to.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The structural embodiment of cosmic imbalance, destruction, and the dark opposition to divine order.

GOLD SEAM: the impersonal cosmic order (Zoroastrian Aṣ̌a / Aša) that truth-tellers embody and liars violate — ọfọ na ọgụ likewise vindicates only the clean and turns upon the false. Righteousness as a self-enforcing law across families.
GOLD SEAM: The cosmic adversary who seeks to ruin humanity, though Islam strictly denies him the co-eternal, dualistic power held by the Zoroastrian destructive spirit.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both categorize the spiritual realm into highly regimented, color-coded hierarchies of divine emissaries that oversee specific earthly domains.

GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme, wise, and just creators who reveal truth through a prophet (Moses/Zarathustra) and demand ethical conduct — but YHWH is a covenant partner with a chosen people, while Ahura Mazda is the source of all good in a dualist cosmos.

GOLD SEAM: Both are the divine order that humans must align with — Torah is the revealed instruction of God, while Aṣ̌a is the cosmic truth that the righteous embody. Both are central to their respective traditions as the path to salvation.

GOLD SEAM: Both are future saviors who will bring about the final redemption — the Jewish Messiah is a descendant of David, while the Zoroastrian Saoshyant is a posthumous son of Zarathustra who will raise the dead and renew the world. Both are eschatological figures.

GOLD SEAM: Both are adversarial forces — but Ha-Satan is a servant of God who tests humans, while Angra Mainyu is the co-eternal evil principle in Zoroastrian dualism. The Jewish Satan is subordinate; the Zoroastrian one is a cosmic opponent. The difference is the key.

GOLD SEAM: Both are the process of making the world good again — teshuvah is individual repentance that transforms sin into merit, while frashokereti is the Zoroastrian renovation that purifies all creation. Both are acts of restoration.

GOLD SEAM: Both are the final state of redemption — the World to Come is the age of eternal life and closeness to God, while frashokereti is the Zoroastrian renewal of the world after the final battle. Both involve resurrection and judgment.
GOLD SEAM: Both are a specific set of divine attributes/emanations through which a transcendent creator interacts with and sustains the world.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions task humanity with an active, ritual role in the ultimate purification and restoration of a fundamentally broken cosmos.
GOLD SEAM: The realm of darkness and destruction, though Kabbalah strips it of co-eternal independence, rendering it a parasitic byproduct of divine judgment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fundamental cosmological principle mapping spatial purity against forces of decay and pollution.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A well-documented historical borrowing; the Ob-Ugric figure assimilated profound solar and horse-riding attributes from ancient Indo-Iranian steppe contacts with Mithra.

Two principled adversaries of order — the black-sky destroyer //Gaunab and the Hostile Spirit of Iran — each the named source of sickness, death, and disorder locked against a benevolent supreme will. A structural twin of opposed-power cosmologies, by independent routes; Khoekhoe dualism is flagged as possibly sharpened by missionary contact.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel—a primordial patriarch given dominion over a specific expanse of earth to establish a golden age of pastoral prosperity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial ancestor and civilizing king whose reign ends following a foundational transgression, leading to a permanent change in the human condition.
GOLD SEAM: A supreme creator of unalloyed light and goodness, standing in absolute metaphysical opposition to the forces of darkness.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme sovereign of the dark, chaotic realm whose sole purpose is to ruin and entrap the creations of Light.
GOLD SEAM: In Sasanian translation, Mani explicitly equated his Prince of Darkness with the Zoroastrian Ahriman to evangelize Iran.

GOLD SEAM: In a radical theological inversion, Middle Persian Manichaeism translated the Primal Man as 'Ohrmazd', casting the supreme Zoroastrian deity as the suffering savior rather than the creator.
GOLD SEAM: The Bema ritually anticipates the final judgment and the total purification of light, mirroring the Zoroastrian renovation of the universe.

GOLD SEAM: Both are solar, covenant-keeping gods who mediate between heaven and earth — but the Roman Mithras is a bull-slaying savior in a cave, while the Iranian Mithra is a judge and warrior of the open sky. The two share a name and some solar traits but diverge radically in myth and cult.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme incarnate figures destined to intervene at the climax of historical degradation, ruthlessly destroying systemic corruption and restoring cosmic moral order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Qormusta is a direct linguistic borrowing of Ohrmazd (Ahura Mazda), transmitted to the steppe via Sogdian merchants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both treat fire as an intensely sacred, purifying living entity that must never be polluted by filth or water.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are uncreated, supreme architects of truth and light who deploy sacred fire as their primary earthly manifestation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are conscious, sacred fires acting as the direct, earthly representatives of a remote, supreme celestial creator.
GOLD SEAM: The fundamental, unyielding law of the cosmos that enforces oaths and punishes transgression, preceding the whims of individual gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both figures serve as the architect of a corrupted counter-creation; Angra Mainyu creates pests and death, while Yacub grafts the 'devil' race.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The impending, apocalyptic purification of the world order through a definitive act of fire and destruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as conscious, holy fires that must be ritually tended and kept pure to transmit prayers to the supreme creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A co-eternal principle of destruction and disease that structurally requires the creator's constant opposition.
GOLD SEAM: Both envision a definitive, linear end-state of cosmic renewal and the banishment of darkness, though the New Age achieves it through frequency shifts, not divine battle.
GOLD SEAM: Zoroastrianism posits a literal, co-eternal principle of evil that must be fought; New Thought strictly denies that evil has any ontological existence at all.
GOLD SEAM: A foundational principle of truth and right-working order that governs the cosmos, which humans must align with to avoid suffering.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While the Saoshyant arrives to perfect the world and end evil, Vindex arrives to violently destroy universalism and install a ruthless, unequal hierarchy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Acausal functions identically to the domain of Angra Mainyu—a realm of pure, destructive, and entropic force fundamentally hostile to the ordered cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An omnipresent, invisible force of pure malice held strictly responsible for all death, disease, and misfortune in an otherwise ordered world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmic adversary to order, though Discordianism inverts the Zoroastrian valence by making the chaotic destroyer the necessary hero.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An ultimate principle of opposition and rebellion, often demonized by orthodox systems but essential to the cosmic tension of the left-hand path.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A legendary sovereign who organizes a desperate migration to save the seeds of civilization from a catastrophic destruction in the homeland.
GOLD SEAM: The prophetic forerunner who heralds the imminent arrival of a final, triumphant divine order that will remake the world.
GOLD SEAM: The overarching system of total negativity, destruction, and spiritual falsehood that wages war against the forces of light.
GOLD SEAM: A decentralized network of luminous, hidden ministers tirelessly executing the will of the cosmic order without seeking earthly glory.
GOLD SEAM: The profound eschatological hope for a final, total healing of the world through the purgation of ignorance and the triumph of light.
GOLD SEAM: The anticipation of a world-renovating force that will synthesize all fragmented truths and eradicate earthly suffering.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A future restorer who arrives to end a dark age, though the Taheb is strictly mortal and restores a specific cult rather than permanently recreating the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve sealing away the seeds of a perfect order inside a hidden subterranean enclosure, to be opened when the age of darkness ends.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit an active principle of truth and order that individuals must continuously choose to align with against falsehood.
GOLD SEAM: A cosmic adversary responsible for the corruption of the original pure state, translating the concept of primeval evil into the figure of a despotic galactic politician.
GOLD SEAM: Shared archaic Indo-Iranian veneration of the sacred, purifying fire as the highest divine principle.
GOLD SEAM: The primordial Indo-Iranian first man who establishes the structure and lineage of human society.
GOLD SEAM: While Zoroastrianism posits an external, co-eternal principle of evil, Sikhism rejects dualism entirely; evil is merely the internal sickness of the ego separating itself from the One.
GOLD SEAM: Roman Mithras takes his name and his fundamental association with solar-contractual authority directly from the Iranian Mithra, despite heavy adaptation in the Roman mystery cult.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme lords of light and cosmic order who grant a specific, divine radiance or 'right to rule' (farr in Iran, solar comes in Rome) to the sovereign king.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both feature a final, purifying cosmic fire, though Stoicism envisions eternal cyclical recurrence rather than a permanent, linear redemption.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Zoroastrian holy fire and the Stoic fiery breath both function as the conscious, purifying, and structuring energy of the divine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unbending, divine truth and righteous order of the cosmos, to which the virtuous individual must actively and willingly conform their mind.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, perfected state of the world where suffering ceases and human existence aligns entirely with the divine will.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Radically rejects the concept of an independent evil cosmic force; suffering is merely the mundane accumulation of human selfishness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The adversarial framework of Satan heavily derives from Zoroastrianism's Angra Mainyu, though Theistic Satanism inverses the moral polarity.
GOLD SEAM: The sacred, internal flame that acts as the active, animating principle of life and cosmic order.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate spiritual adversary, though in Thelema it is a force of psychological dispersion rather than an independent evil creator.
GOLD SEAM: A cosmic, fiery principle that acts as the active, animating bridge maintaining the vital order of the universe.
GOLD SEAM: Equestrian/martial savior figures who slay beasts, guarantee oaths, and dominate the esoteric cultic landscape of soldiers in the Roman era.
GOLD SEAM: Primordial figures who secure a subterranean refuge or paradise to preserve the righteous and grant immortality away from surface destruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The human scientists of Cosmism fulfill the exact structural role of the Zoroastrian Saoshyant—the agents who act in the end-times to physically resurrect the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a definitive eschatological rupture where the current, flawed world order is permanently replaced by a perfected, immortal existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cryonics acts as a modern 'Vara' of Yima—a fortified, frozen vault designed to preserve the seeds of life through a fatal winter until the world is safe again.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize death not as a part of the divine balance, but as a hostile, invasive corruption of the world that must be actively fought and permanently destroyed.

FLAGGED reach: both promise a deathless, evil-free world — but Frashokereti is the cosmic Making-Wonderful at the end of time, an eschatological renovation, while the Land-Without-Evil is a place reached by WALKING in the living body. The shared longing for a world without death, sought versus awaited.
GOLD SEAM: Both feature a strict hierarchy of radiant, benevolent entities actively assisting the supreme order in a cosmic battle against dark, chaotic forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The requirement that spiritual truth must be tangibly operationalized through right action, overriding any internal passive faith.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The use of an unquenchable flame not to consume sacrifices, but to serve as the radiant, physical anchor of truth, purity, and communal presence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A powerful adversarial figure whose choices introduced chaos, quarantine, and spiritual decay into an otherwise ordered creation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A grand, future-facing cosmic trajectory where the universe and its inhabitants are actively working toward a finalized state of perfection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both feature a primordial heptad of divine emanations governing creation, reflecting a deeply documented historical continuum in Iranian religious thought.
GOLD SEAM: Connects to the Iranian supreme creator of unalloyed good, devoid of any inherent darkness.
GOLD SEAM: Tawûsê Melek is the ultimate inversion of the Zoroastrian dark spirit—accused of being the adversary by outsiders, but structurally representing pure loyalty and the extinguishing of hell.
GOLD SEAM: A fundamental heptad of divine beings emanating directly from the supreme God, tasked with maintaining the physical and spiritual order of the universe.
GOLD SEAM: The central Near Eastern figure of solar light, overseeing oaths, purity, and the visible manifestation of divine truth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A definitive, linear historical schema culminating in a final cosmic rescue and the purification of the world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The final eschatological savior tasked with rescuing the righteous at the closing of the age.
Within Iran: Ahura Mazda is the lord OF Asa; the Wise Lord and the Truth he upholds are inseparable in the Gathas.
The primal twin Spirits of Yasna 30 who each chose — the structural core of the dualism, a pairing that is war not balance.
Angra Mainyu is the partisan of Druj (the Lie), the exact inverse of Asa (Truth) — the dualism is fundamentally Truth versus Lie.
One-through-many within Iran: the six Bounteous Immortals are facets of the single Wise Lord, the One read as a council of virtues.
The Saoshyant is the agent who brings about the Frashokereti — saviour and renovation are one eschatological event.

Flagship cosmic-order cognate: Avestan asa / Old Persian arta and Vedic rta both descend from PIE *h2r-to. THE brightest gold seam to Vedic — but note: no 'rta' figure id is listed; seam realised via brahman/cosmic-order joint.
Both solar guardians, but the true cognate is to Mitra not Surya; here a functional solar parallel only.

Avestan Yima and Vedic Yama descend from a common Indo-Iranian *Yama, 'twin' — but Iran keeps him as FIRST KING and ark-builder while India makes him first to DIE and lord of the dead: a cognate that inverts function.
The cognate's divergence: Iran's Yima preserves life through the winter; India's Yama is the first mortal and king of the underworld. Same name, opposite valence of death.

The great Ahura/Asura inversion: Avestan 'ahura' and Vedic 'asura' are cognate, but Iran exalts the ahuras as good and demonises the daevas, while India exalts the devas and demonises the asuras — cognate words, flipped valence. (No standalone asura figure id listed; seam noted home.)
Both inherit the Indo-Iranian high-sky lordship, but Ahura Mazda is moralised into an ethical creator-absolute rather than a passive Father Sky.
Long-argued kinship of the Wise Lord with the Vedic asura Varuna, sovereign guardian of rta and the oath — structurally the closest Vedic match. (Varuna not in listed ids; noted as scholarly link.)

Tier-1 Indo-Iranian cognate: Avestan Mithra and Vedic Mitra, both gods of the covenant/contract, from a shared Indo-Iranian deity of the binding bond. (Mitra not in listed ids; the seam's home is the Vedic shard.)

The hinge of the whole mirror: Iran takes the paired-opposites structure and makes it WAR (good must defeat evil), where China makes it harmony (each contains and becomes the other). Dualism is complementarity refused.
Where Yin is not evil but the necessary complement of Yang, Ahriman is evil that ought not to exist — the moral asymmetry that separates dualism from balance.
Both are principled adversaries woven into the cosmos, but Tezcatlipoca is ambivalent fate-and-smoke while Ahriman is unambiguous evil — the contrast sharpens what ethical dualism means.
Both bring death into a good creation, but Loki is a trickster-insider who turns, whereas Angra Mainyu is the adversary by original choice — chaos versus principled evil.
Resurrection of the dead, final judgement and a world made new — Zoroastrian eschatology is widely held to have shaped Second-Temple and Christian apocalyptic during Persian rule over Judea.
The virgin-born future redeemer who raises the dead at the end of days — a documented likely influence on the messianic template, via the Achaemenid/Parthian contact with Judaism.

The principled Adversary as a cosmic figure (ha-satan grown into a true Devil) enters Abrahamic thought during and after the Babylonian exile under Persian dominion — historical contact, contested degree.
Yima's vara built to outlast a world-ending winter rhymes with Noah's ark before the flood — a shared flood-survival/ark motif, no descent claimed.
Both center sacred fire, but Iran's Atar is the kept witness of Truth, not the stolen gift of the culture-hero — fire as worship versus fire as theft.
Indo-Iranian fire-cult kinship: both tend a divine sacrificial fire as messenger between worlds, though Iran refuses to treat the flame as a devouring idol. (Agni not in listed ids.)
Both are the impersonal right-order the cosmos obeys, but Asa is moralised into a Truth one must fight FOR, while the Dao is the order one yields TO.
Asa as cosmic Truth shares the cosmic-order joint with the Vedic absolute; the firmer cognate, though, is Asa to rta, not to Brahman.
The One refracted into emanated aspects/virtues — a structural rhyme with the sefirot flowing from Ein Sof, the unknowable refracting into nameable powers.
Two near-monotheist creator-lords of Truth in historical contact under the Achaemenids; the structural and possibly genetic kinship of ethical monotheism.

Child heresy of the Zoroastrian two-spirits doctrine: Mazdak's blind, accidental Darkness is Ahriman demoted from strategist to stumbling force — a reform reading (drist-dēn) from inside the Zoroastrian fold, known only through its enemies.
The enthroned Lord of Light is Ohrmazd re-imagined through the Sasanian court — the same supreme good deity, re-furnished with four Powers, seven viziers, and twelve spiritual forces.
GOLD SEAM: The Zurvanite twin-myth is direct exegesis of Yasna 30.3, where the two primal Spirits are 'twins' — the heresy grows from the parent scripture's own unanswered question: twins of whom?
GOLD SEAM: Zurvanism demotes the uncreated Wise Lord into a created son of Time — orthodoxy's supreme god becomes one of two brothers, a reading the Pahlavi books polemicize against.
GOLD SEAM: The fixed term of the cosmic battle ending in the renovation is the shared Zoroastrian clock — Zurvanism keeps orthodoxy's ending intact while re-parenting its combatants.
Sacred fire treated as an active divine force at the center of both cosmic order and household ritual, requiring perpetual ceremonial maintenance.
Both are a world-renewing figure prophesied to appear at history's end and complete what the current dispensation could not — independently arrived at in Zoroastrian and Buddhist eschatology.

Typological only: fire tended in a dedicated installation as a ritual center — no historical descent claimed between the contested Kalibangan pits and the later, textually attested Zoroastrian fire cult.
Both are awaited restorer-figures who set the world right at history's end — a structural analogy long noted in comparative eschatology, no direct descent claimed here.

Two final purifying-fire eschatologies that close cosmic history and end the struggle between light/dark or good/evil — the Great Fire's 1,468-year conflagration against Frashokereti's molten-metal ordeal.

cosmic ages ending in cataclysm and issuing a renewed world — though Frashokereti is Ahura Mazda's morally resolved final victory over evil, while Ragnarök is a fated, mutual slaughter with no clean victor.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both are named, chief entities whose ascendance is definitionally hostile to the ordered cosmos — though Atazoth is chief of a plural pantheon of Dark Gods, where Ahriman is Zoroastrianism's sole co-eternal principle of evil.
Avestan/Zoroastrian Yima continues *Yemo by the same etymon as Vedic Yama, though Iranian myth transforms the sacrificed twin into a golden-age king rather than a slain co-founder.
the awaited restorer who arrives at history's worst point to defeat evil and end the current age, held at structural tier given Zoroastrian and Vaishnava eschatology's independent development.

Ṛta and Avestan Aša continue one reconstructed Indo-Iranian word for cosmic truth-order — ennobled as the highest good on the Iranian side, this shard's Varuna already carries the parallel to Ahura Mazda.
The Wise Lord reveals himself to Zarathushtra in the Gathas' visionary dialogues (Yasna 28-34) — prophet and god bound in a single act of revelation.
Every Saoshyant to come is held to be born of Zarathushtra's own seed, miraculously preserved in a lake — founder and final savior are one bloodline stretched across all of history.
The Gathas, the Avesta's ancient core, are the prophet's own attributed words; the rest of the corpus grew up around them.
The tradition's foundational opposition stated directly: Asa is Truth, Druj is the Lie, and every being is sorted as ashavan or drugvant by which it serves.
Angra Mainyu is Druj's champion and chief partisan — the Destroying Spirit is the will that chose the Lie, Druj is the falsehood he chose.
The Bundahishn names Azi Dahaka among Angra Mainyu's chief monstrous creations, mightiest of the Lie's creatures set against the good creation.
Sraosha meets and guides the soul at the Bridge on the third night after death — the psychopomp and the threshold he presides over.
Sraosha (Obedience) and Aeshma (Wrath) are named direct opposites in the Gathas — the disciplined hearer of the divine word against the daeva of blind fury.

Both are collectively honored guardian-dead who protect the living lineage and receive periodic communal rites — but Iran's Fravashis pre-exist birth by cosmic choice, while the amadlozi become ancestors only after death.

Both battle a chaos-force to release the waters — Tishtrya wrestles the drought-demon Apaosha as horse against horse, Indra slays the serpent Vrtra outright; a shared Indo-Iranian rain-battle structure, resolved differently.

Avestan Verethragna and Vedic Vrtrahan (Indra's own epithet as 'smiter of Vrtra') descend from the same Indo-Iranian compound *vrtra-ghna — Iran promotes the epithet into an independent god of Victory.

Aeshma Daeva passes by name directly into Second Temple Jewish demonology as Ashmedai, then into the medieval grimoire tradition as King Asmoday — a traceable chain from Gathic daeva to ceremonial-magic demon.

Both are water/fertility goddesses bound up with royal power and warfare; Berossus records the Achaemenid court under Artaxerxes II explicitly linking Anahita's new cult statues to Ishtar's, though the identification was a political program, not a native Iranian one.
The pressed Haoma and the tended Fire are the twin centers of the Yasna liturgy — the drunk sacrament and the witnessed flame, the two elements no Zoroastrian rite proceeds without.
The exposure of the dead exists to protect the same good creations — fire, earth and water — that Atar is the visible witness of; purity law and fire-cult are one logic seen from two rites.
The Zurvanite myth names its dark twin outright: Ahriman is the being conceived from Zurvan's momentary doubt, tearing his way from the womb first to seize the promised kingship.

dossier.essence: One of the oldest continuously practiced revealed religions, founded by the Iranian prophet Zarathushtra (Zoroaster), dated by most linguists to roughly 1200-1000 BCE, and long the dominant faith of the pre-Islamic Iranian empires until the Muslim conquest of the 7th century CE. It centers on Ahura Mazda, the one wholly good creator-God, and on the cosmic and moral struggle between Truth (asha) and the Lie (druj). It survives among the Parsis of India and the Zoroastrians (Iranis) of Iran and the diaspora.
dossier.cosmos: Ahura Mazda, the uncreated Wise Lord, is opposed by a hostile spirit, Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), who freely chose evil; the good creation is invaded, and cosmic history is a bounded, forward-moving battle, arguably the first true linear eschatology, that the good is guaranteed to win. Ahura Mazda works through the Amesha Spentas, six 'Bounteous Immortals' (Good Mind, Truth, Dominion, Devotion, Wholeness, Immortality). Each soul is judged after death at the Chinvat Bridge; at the end of time comes the Frashokereti, the 'making wonderful,' when the savior Saoshyant leads the resurrection of the dead, a river of molten metal refines all souls, and the world is restored to deathless perfection.
dossier.practice: The ethical core is 'good thoughts, good words, good deeds' and the active choice of asha over druj, with ritual purity paramount. Worship, led by a hereditary priesthood (mobeds), centers on the sacred fire, tended in fire-temples as the visible emblem of Truth and never worshipped as an idol, and on recitation of the Avesta, especially the Yasna liturgy. Distinctive rites include investiture with the sacred shirt and cord (sudreh and kusti) and the traditional exposure of the dead in 'towers of silence' (dakhma) to avoid polluting earth and fire; membership is by birth.
dossier.canon: The Avesta, in the Avestan language, whose oldest stratum is the Gathas attributed to Zarathushtra himself (embedded in the Yasna), with the Yashts (hymns to individual divinities) and the Vendidad; later Middle Persian (Pahlavi) works such as the Bundahishn and Denkard preserve much of the cosmology and commentary.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds evil as a moral choice and history as a moral arrow, the clearest statement that good and evil are principled adversaries and that time is going somewhere, ending in judgment and renewal.
sources: Mary Boyce, 'Zoroastrians: Their Religious Beliefs and Practices' (1979)
Michael Stausberg & Yuhan Sohrab-Dinshaw Vevaina (eds.), 'The Wiley Blackwell Companion to Zoroastrianism' (2015)
